coredata.dc:title: Supported carbon dots decorated with metallothionein for selective cadmium adsorption and removal
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Ming-Li Chen. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Ming-Li Chen. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Ting
Li, Yi-Kun
Chen, Ming-Li
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are prepared and immobilized onto spherical SiO2 through a one-step thermal oxidation and then decorated with metallothionein (MT), a protein with high affinity towards thiophilic metals. The MT-carbon dots composites are characterized by means of FT-IR, SEM and TGA, giving rise to a MT loading amount of 823μgg−1. The adsorption of cadmium by the composites is a fast process and follows Langmuir model. In comparison with native SiO2, a 2- and 2.4-folds improvement on the static and dynamic adsorption capacity of the composites for cadmium are obtained, respectively. Moreover, the adsorption efficiency is not affected by the presence of other metals. Finally, the composites are successfully applied for the removal of cadmium in a series of environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cadmium
Adsorption
Metallothionein
Heavy metal remediation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of MT functionalized supported C-dots
Fabrication of supported C-dots and MT decoration
Enhancement on cadmium adsorption capacity and selectivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Scheme of the fabrication of supported C-dots decorated by MT. (B) Flow manifold of the sequential injection system for conducting on-line adsorption of cadmium by MT-CD@SiO2. SP, syringe pump; HC, holding coil; W, waste; C, microcolumn.
(A) Images of amorphous SiO2 (a and d), C18 resin (b and e) and spherical SiO2 (c and f) before (a–c) and after (d–f) C-dots attachment (40×). (B) 3D fluorescence spectra of the supported C-dots.
(A) SEM images (500×), (B) FT-IR spectra and (C) Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) of (a) SiO2, (b) SiO2 supported C-dots (CD@SiO2) and (c) supported C-dots decorated with MT (MT-CD@SiO2).

Effect of pH (A) and time (B) on cadmium sorption efficiency by MT-CD@SiO2. Condition in A: on-line adsorption; Cd2+: 100μgL−1, 1000μL; sample loading flow rate: 10μLs−1; adsorbent: 6mg. Condition in B: batch mode; Cd2+: 100μgL−1, pH=6.0, 8mL; adsorbents: 32mg;

The (A) adsorption isotherms and (B) breakthrough curves for cadmium by SiO2 and MT-CD@SiO2. Condition in A: 1mL of Cd2+ solution (initial concentration range from 0.1 to 10mgL−1, pH=6.0) under vigorous stirring for 90min; adsorbent: 4mg. Condition in B: Cd2+: 100μgL−1, pH=6.0; sample loading flow rate: 5μLs−1; adsorbent for column packing: 6mg.
The comparison of Langmuir constant for SiO2 and MT-CD@SiO2.
The effect of co-existing ions on the removal of cadmium (100μgL−1) using SiO2 and MT-CD@SiO2 as adsorbent (Mean±S.D., n =2–3).
The removal efficiency of cadmium spiked in a series of environmental waters using MT-CD@SiO2 as adsorbent (Mean±S.D., n =2–3).

head.ce:title: Supported carbon dots decorated with metallothionein for selective cadmium adsorption and removal

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A selective and efficient adsorbent for cadmium is prepared by one-step synthesis of supported carbon dots followed by the decoration with metallothionein.

Carbon dots are prepared and immobilized onto spherical SiO2 through a one-step thermal oxidation and then decorated with metallothionein (MT), a protein with high affinity towards thiophilic metals. The MT-carbon dots composites are characterized by means of FT-IR, SEM and TGA, giving rise to a MT loading amount of 823μgg−1. The adsorption of cadmium by the composites is a fast process and follows Langmuir model. In comparison with native SiO2, a 2- and 2.4-folds improvement on the static and dynamic adsorption capacity of the composites for cadmium are obtained, respectively. Moreover, the adsorption efficiency is not affected by the presence of other metals. Finally, the composites are successfully applied for the removal of cadmium in a series of environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Known as the cause for Japan Itai-Itai disease, cadmium is among the most toxic heavy metals in surface water and soil. The total production of cadmium worldwide is estimated to be 22,300t according to the British Geological Survey [1]. Besides natural release of cadmium through the weathering of rocks, forest fire and volcanoes, human activity accounts for almost half of the cadmium production, including manufacturing of batteries, pigments, plastic stabilizers, metal plating, and phosphate fertilizers [1,2]. Chronic accumulation of cadmium in human bodies causes the damage in kidney, liver, cardiovascular and skeletal systems, while high dosage uptake causes Itai-Itai disease or even death [3]. Cadmium is also identified as a human carcinogen [4]. Due to its detrimental effect to human health, the permissible cadmium concentration of WHO [5] and EPA [6] guidelines in drinking water is strictly restricted to below 0.003 and 0.005mgL−1, respectively.

Various physical and chemical approaches have been employed for cadmium removal, including precipitation, ion exchange, membrane separation and adsorption [1]. Among them, adsorption techniques stand out from other approaches with advantages of simplicity, favorable efficiency, and cost-effective. Conventional sorbents such as active carbon, zeolite, and metal oxides, are usually employed for the adsorption and removal of heavy metal species including cadmium. However, they always suffer from drawbacks of low capacity or limited efficiency.

Carbon based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene and their derivates, are the most promising sorbents for several organic pollutants and heavy metal species owing to their large specific surface area, abundant binding sites and mechanical stability [7]. The applications of carbon based nanomaterials as heavy metal adsorbent are massively reviewed [8–14]. Metal adsorption capacity of CNTs is directly related to their total active surface acidity [10]. Oxidizing CNTs with H2O2, KMnO4 and HNO3 increased the adsorption capacity of cadmium mainly due to the increased oxygen-containing functional groups [15]. The preparation of graphene oxide (GO) from graphite using Hummers methods introduces more oxygen-containing groups thus generally absorbs more metal cations than non-functionalized CNTs [16]. However, for the case of GO nano-sheets, their immobilization on either magnetic core for magnetic separation [17] or solid support for on-line column packing [18] is necessary for the isolation of GO from aqueous medium. Meanwhile, the high cost of both CNTs and GO restricts their application in environmental protection [19]. Besides, neither CNTs nor GO prefers to adsorb cadmium rather than other heavy metal cations, thus functionalization procedure is necessary to improve the adsorption selectivity [19,20]. Metallothioneins (MTs) are low molecular weight (6–7kDa) of cysteine-rich proteins that exhibit high capacity for metal chelation [21]. Our previous work has shown an obvious improvement on adsorption selectivity towards cadmium after surface decoration of immobilized GO with Cyanobacterium MTs [22]. The tolerance level for co-existing ions especially alkali and alkaline earth metals has been significantly improved up to 800,000 folds after MT decoration.

As the latest member in nano carbon family, carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted much attention due to their unique photoluminescence properties [23,24], but the adsorption potential has not been much exploited yet. In fact, carbon dots possess more oxygen-containing groups in comparison with its siblings, thus could potentially have higher metal adsorption capacity. Besides, unlike CNTs and graphene, the simple and economic preparation steps make the large scale production and commercialization of C-dots possible. By thermal oxidation of proper precursors on solid supports such as zeolite [25] the preparation and immobilization of C-dots could be facilitated in one step, resulting in an ideal sorbent with suitable mechanic strength and versatile binding surface for various targets.

In the present work, metallothionein isolated from rabbit liver is decorated onto the surface of supported C-dots to form a novel sorbent for the selective adsorption and removal of cadmium. The adsorption behaviors of cadmium, including the pH-dependent adsorption, the sorption capacity and selectivity, the sorption kinetics and isotherms, are investigated. This novel composite is successfully applied for the removal of cadmium in environmental waters.

A GGX-200 atomic absorption spectrometer (Beijing Hai-Guang Instrument Co. Ltd, Beijing, China) with deuterium background correction is employed. A cadmium hollow cathode lamp (General Research Institute for non-ferrous metals, Beijing, China) is used as light source at a wavelength of 228.8nm and operated at 5mA, with a 0.4nm slit width. Pyrolytically coated graphite tubes are used. Peak area (integrated absorbance) values are adopted for quantification. The GFAAS temperature program for the detection of cadmium is given herein (ramp/holding time): drying at 100°C (15s/10s), pyrolysis at 350°C (15s/10s), atomization at 1900°C (1s/3s), and cleaning at 2100°C (1s/3s).

A FIAlab 3000 sequential injection system (FIAlab Instruments Inc., Medina, WA, USA) equipped with a 24,000-step syringe pump (Cavro, Sunnyvale, CA, USA, with a 2.5mL syringe) and a 6-port selection valve is employed for fluidic delivery and sample processing.

Unless otherwise specified, all the chemicals used in the present study are analytical reagent grade obtained from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Metallothionein (MT) isolated from rabbit liver is purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Spherical SiO2 particles (75–150μm in diameter, Makall Group, Qingdao, China) are dried at 120°C for 2h before use.

A stock solution of cadmium (1000mgL−1) is prepared by dissolving appropriate amount of cadmium pellet in 1.0molL−1 HNO3. Working standards of various concentrations are prepared by serial dilution of the stock solution. pH value of the sample solution is adjusted to pH 6.0 by using 0.1molL−1 HNO3 and/or 0.1molL−1 NaOH. Deionized (DI) water (18.2MΩcm) is used throughout.
One-step preparation of supported C-dots is achieved by thermal oxidization of the homogeneous mixture of SiO2 spherical particles and the precursor to C-dots, as illustrated in Fig. 1A.

The preparation of precursor to C-dots: Briefly, 2g of H2N(CH2)10COOH is dissolved in 25mL of DI water, followed by the addition of 0.45g NaOH. 25mL of citric acid (8% (m/v)) is then added slowly under vigorous stirring. Thereafter, the white precipitate is collected, washed and dried at room temperature for 24h and followed by further drying at 85°C for 2h. The dried precipitate (the precursor) is grounded to a uniform powder prior to use for the next step.

One-step preparation of supported C-dots: The precursor (1g) and spherical SiO2 (1g) are well mixed and placed in an alumina crucible and then heated at 300°C for 2h with a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1. The unattached C-dots are removed by filtering with 100μm meshes and repeated washing with DI water. The prepared SiO2 supported C-dots (CD@SiO2 for short) is dried at 85°C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of MT functionalized supported C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The functionalization of supported C-dots with MT is accomplished via EDC/EHS coupling. A two-step attachment process is employed to reduce the self-coupling between proteins. 100mg of supported C-dots is added in 10mL of morpholineethanesulfonic acid (MES) buffer (500mmol L−1, pH 6.0), followed by addition of 20mL of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) solution (10mgmL−1) and 20mL of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl ammonium propyl) carbon imine hydrochloride (EDC) solution (50mgmL−1). The mixture is then stirred at room temperature for 1 h. Unreacted EDC and NHS are removed by repeated centrifugation (10,000rpm, 5min) and washing with MES buffer.

In the second step, the supported C-dots are re-dispersed in PBS buffer (100mmol/L, pH 7.4) and 1mg of MT is then added. The mixture is stirred overnight (avoid light, 20°C). The MT decorated CD@SiO2 are washed with DDW until no protein is detected in the eluate. The MT decorated CD@SiO2 are labeled as MT-CD@SiO2 hereafter.

MT-CD@SiO2 is characterized by FT-IR, TGA and SEM. FT-IR spectra are obtained using a Nicolet-6700 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Instruments Inc, USA) from 500cm−1 to 4000cm−1. TGA is performed on a thermogravimetric analyzer (Mettler Toledo, Sweden), with a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 in N2. SEM images are taken on an SSX-550 scanning electron microscope (Shimadzu, Japan). The UV/vis spectral measurements are carried out by using a U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan). The fluorescence spectra are recorded by a F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan).

Batch adsorption kinetics for cadmium are evaluated as following: 32mg of the MT-CD@SiO2 composite is added to 8mL of 100μgL−1 Cd2+ solution under magnetic stirring. The residue Cd2+ in the supernatant is quantified by GFAAS at appropriate time intervals.

For the purpose of determining dynamic breakthrough capacity, a 100μgL−1 Cd2+ solution is continuously directed to flow through the mini-column loaded with 6mg of MT-CD@SiO2 composite using SIA system at a flow rate of 5μLs−1 until a complete breakthrough occurs. The effluent is collected in 1000μL aliquots and the residue cadmium concentration is determined by GFAAS. The flow manifold for determining dynamic breakthrough capacity is illustrated in Fig. 1B.

The adsorption isotherms are evaluated at room temperature and in batch conditions. A mixture containing 4mg of MT-CD@SiO2 composite and 1mL of Cd2+ (ranging from 0.1 to 10mgL−1, pH 6.0) is shaken vigorously for 90min. The cadmium concentration at equilibrium in the supernatant is quantified by GFAAS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of supported C-dots and MT decoration
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots can be roughly divided into dispersed C-dots and supported C-dots. The dispersed one is usually hard to separate from aqueous medium through centrifugation due to its superior solubility and nano size. The supported C-dots inherit the mechanic strength and macro-size from its solid support, thus is particularly suitable for on line column adsorption and separation for targets.

Amorphous SiO2, spherical SiO2 particles (75–150μm) and C18 resin beads (40–60μm) are compared as solid support for one-step thermal preparation of supported C-dots. It is interesting to note that only the C-dots on spherical SiO2 show good uniformity under microscope (Fig. 2A), which is probably due to the favorable surface energy of spherical shape. Meanwhile, C-dots are poorly attached on the amorphous SiO2, and the tiny size of C18 beads makes it difficult to separate C18 supported C-dots and agglomerated ones. Therefore, spherical SiO2 particles are chosen as solid support for C-dots. The C-dots is firmly attached on the surface of SiO2, they are not stripped off even in acidic medium or by vigorous shaking. The amount of C-dots loaded on SiO2 through thermal preparation is also much more than that obtained through physical adsorption. Fig. 2B shows the fluorescence property of the C-dots stripped off the CD@SiO2 by vigorous ultrasonic stripping the CD@SiO2 in alkali solution. The C-dots solution shows a maximum emission wavelength at 430nm when excited at 350nm. As for C-dots on SiO2, however, the fluorescence is quenched mainly due to self-quenching.

MT is further decorated on the surface of CD@SiO2 through a two-step di-imide-activated amidation process, in the hope of endowing the adsorption selectivity towards cadmium. Instead of maintaining the reaction system in the MES buffer (pH 6.0) as previously reported, PBS buffer solution is employed as reaction medium which leads to ca. 4-fold increase on the protein loading. SEM, FT-IR and TGA are employed for the characterization of MT decorated CD@SiO2.

The morphological structure of spherical SiO2, CD@SiO2 and MT-CD@SiO2 is compared by SEM. As shown in Fig. 3A, a rougher surface of CD@SiO2 compared to spherical SiO2 clearly indicates the successful loading of C-dots. As for MT-CD@SiO2, the loading layer on the sphere surface gets thicker, resulted from MT decoration.

The FT-IR bands of SiO2 at 798cm−1 and 1099cm−1 are attributed to SiOH and SiOSi stretching vibration, respectively (Fig. 3B(a)), while the presence of CO (ν =1400cm−1), CO (ν =1627cm−1) and CH2 (ν =2852cm−1 and 2933cm−1) in the FT-IR spectra of CD@SiO2 confirms the existence of C-dots on silica surface (Fig. 3B(b)). As for MT-CD@SiO2 (Fig. 3B(c)), the new absorption band at 1629cm−1 corresponds to amide I (the CO stretch of MT) and that at 1573cm−1 is assigned to amide II (the CN stretch and CNH deformation), whereas the carboxyl CO stretching at 1400cm−1 disappeared with respect to CD@SiO2, indicating the binding of MT to the supported C-dots surface.

The thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA, Fig. 3C) of CD@SiO2 shows a H2O evaporation weight loss below 100°C and another sharp weight loss at around 400–500°C which is presumably due to the loss of CO and CO2 from the oxygen-containing functional groups. The TGA results of MT-CD@SiO2 also shows two steps of weight loss, and the main weight loss (cal. 20%) occurred at 300°C, attributed to the decomposition of the MT, providing further evidence of the presence of MT on supported C-dots.

The loading of MT onto CD@SiO2 is calculated by measuring the SH groups after protein decoration using an Ellman's assay [26]. Based on the amino acid sequences reported [27], the amount of MT loaded is calculated to be 823μgg−1 CD@SiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement on cadmium adsorption capacity and selectivity
ce:section.ce:para: pH Dependence of Cd(II) adsorption by MT-CD@SiO2: pH value of the adsorption medium exhibits pronounced influence on both the dissociation of protein and the distribution of various species of the target metal, thus plays a crucial role in the process of heavy metal biosorption. In the case of cadmium, it precipitates in a basic medium. Thus, the effect of pH value on the biosorption of cadmium by MT-CD@SiO2 composites is investigated within pH 1–9. As shown in Fig. 4A, maximum adsorption occurs at pH 5–9 for cadmium binding. The unfavorable retention of cadmium at lower pH values might be attributed to the competitive binding with active sites on the surface of MT-CD@SiO2 by proton (H+).

Adsorption kinetics and isotherms: The results of batch experiments carried out to evaluate the uptake kinetics of Cd(II) on MT-CD@SiO2 illustrate that the adsorption kinetics is a fast process, and over 90% of Cd(II) is absorbed within 20min (Fig. 4B). The adsorption reaches equilibrium at 40min. The data fit a pseudo-second order kinetic with r 2 values of 0.9999, together with the best-fits rate constants of 2163h−1.

The adsorption capacity of cadmium by MT-CD@SiO2 is investigated by static adsorption equilibrium experiment. Different ranges of cadmium solutions (pH 6.0) are adsorbed by MT-CD@SiO2 under vigorous stir for 90min to facilitate sorption equilibrium. Cd2+ adsorption by MT-CD@SiO2 fits Langmuir model as expressed in the following equation (Fig. 5A).
C e
Q e
=
1
b ×
Q
max
+
C e
Q
max
Q max (μgg−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity, C eq (mgL−1) represents the equilibrium concentration, Q
e is the adsorption capacity, b is the Langmuir constant related to the affinity between the adsorbent and sorbate (Lmg−1). The calculated parameters, e.g., Q max, b and r 2, are shown in Table 1 . The static adsorption capacity of cadmium by MT-CD@SiO2 is 303μgg−1, which is 2 times of the adsorption capacity by SiO2 (151μgg−1).

Dynamic adsorption performance: The dynamic adsorption capacity, derived from the breakthrough curve obtained as described in Section 2 is shown in Fig. 5B, where C 0 and C are the respective concentrations of cadmium in the original solution and the effluent. The breakthrough curve is influenced by various experimental factors, including the initial concentration, flow rate, and the particle size of the absorbent. Under the experimental conditions, the dynamic capacity for Cd(II) is 233μgg−1, which is 2.4 times of the adsorption capacity by SiO2 (96μgg−1). The dynamic capacity towards cadmium is comparable with respect to static capacity, making MT-CD@SiO2 suitable for continuous on-line removal of cadmium.

Adsorption selectivity: It is shown in previous literature that multi-walled CNTs (treated with nitric acid) preferentially adsorbs Pb2+ and Cu2+ rather than Cd2+ [19], while the affinities of GO for metal cations follow the order of Pb2+ >Cu2+ ≫Cd2+ >Zn2+ [20]. However, according to our observations, the adsorption efficiency of Cd2+ (100μgL−1) is barely affected by the presence of 10,000 folds of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and 50 folds of Al3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+ (Table 2 ). The selectivity towards Cd2+ attributes from both oxygen containing groups on C-dots surface and decoration of MT.

The usefulness of MT-CD@SiO2 as cadmium adsorbent is examined by cadmium removal in a series of environmental waters. Because there's no detectable cadmium in these water samples, 10μgL−1 and 100μgL−1 of cadmium is first spiked in the water samples and then adsorbed by 6mg of MT-CD@SiO2. As can be seen in Table 3 , even 100μgL−1 of cadmium can be removed to <4μgL−1, which is below the permissible cadmium concentration of the EPA guidelines. For the purpose of metal recovery, ∼63% of the retained Cd2+ can be steadily recovered by l-Cysteine (1%, w/v) in a nitric acid (0.1M) medium.

In this study, SiO2 supported carbon dots is prepared by one step thermal oxidation and then decorated with metallothionein for selective adsorption and removal of cadmium. The fabrication approach is simple and cost effective, which has the potential for large scale preparation of cadmium adsorbent. The adsorption of cadmium by the composites is effective and efficient, with a comparable dynamic capacity with respect to static capacity, making the composites suitable for continuous on-line removal of cadmium. The successful removal of cadmium in environmental waters expands the scope of application of carbon dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375013, 21235001, 21405010), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Nos. N130105002, N140504003, N140505003, N141008001) are highly appreciated.
